description: A registry MUST NOT persist a publish request whose signature is invalid, even when the content_hash is correct (i.e., even when step 4 of RFC-ACDP-0003 §2.1 passes). This is a negative fixture targeting registry implementations: it pins the normative requirement that the §2.1 pipeline (cryptographic validation steps 1–7) and persistence (steps 8–12) are atomically coupled — a 'verified-except-signature' publish path is non-conformant. See the warning box in RFC-ACDP-0003 §2.1.

input.scenario: A PublishRequest where the content_hash recomputes correctly (step 4 would pass) but signature.value does not verify against the resolved key (step 7 fails). The body content is otherwise structurally well-formed.
input.notes: An implementation that returns invalid_signature but has already written the body to its persistent store fails this fixture. Implementations MUST be tested by inspecting the registry's stored state after the publish attempt — observing the wire response alone is insufficient because both conformant and non-conformant registries return invalid_signature.

post_failure_invariants: Subsequent GET /contexts/{ctx_id} for any ctx_id the registry might have minted from this request MUST return not_found (404).
The body MUST NOT appear in any subsequent GET /contexts/search response, regardless of requester or visibility scoping.

Idempotency-Key state (RFC-ACDP-0003 §6) MUST NOT record a successful publish for this (agent_id, key) pair; a retry with the same key and a corrected signature MUST be processed as a fresh publish, not a 200 replay.
expected.rationale: Hash integrity without signature integrity is incomplete verification. A registry that persists before signature verification is exploitable: an attacker who can craft a valid hash for a forged body (the hash is computed by the registry; only signing requires a key the attacker does not have) gets the body stored under the victim's agent_id even though signature verification will later fail.
